title: 7.6 practice
description: Practice what you learned in lesson 7.6!
topic_path: The Long Nineteenth Century (1750 to 1914 CE)
Comparing Industrialization in Egypt and Japan | 7.6

assessment_items.question_content:
**Why is the Meiji Restoration considered more of a *revolution* than just a restoration?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It pushed Japan to modernize quickly, breaking with past resistance.
It destroyed the emperor’s role, which had been central in Japan for centuries.
It rejected previous efforts to industrialize Japan.
It ended all trade agreements and cut off foreign relationships.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Even after the Meiji Restoration, what challenge continued to put Japan at a disadvantage in industrializing?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: lack of raw materials
limited access to trade
anti-industrial government policies
Japan’s distant location

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following best describes Japan’s society and economy under the Tokugawa Shogunate (1603–1868)?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Japan had a strict social hierarchy and limited contact with foreign countries.
The Tokugawa Shogunate promoted democracy and gave political power to merchants.
Japan encouraged industrialization and global trade to grow its economy.
Japan had a relaxed social structure, with easy movement between social classes.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What was the main reason the United States sent Commodore Matthew Perry and his warships to Japan in 1853?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: to encourage Japan to trade with America
to provide support for Japan’s war against China
to protect American missionaries living in Japan
to help Japan build a modern navy

assessment_items.question_content:
**Why was Japan reluctant to open its ports to foreign trade when Commodore Matthew Perry arrived in 1853?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Japan feared foreign influence and had followed a policy of isolation for over 200 years.
Japan was experiencing a civil war and could not focus on trade issues.
Japan wanted to launch its own overseas empire before opening to trade.
Japan’s international trade was already strong and they rejected U.S. influence.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Why is Japan’s industrialization during the Meiji era often called “defensive modernization”?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Japan hoped to avoid colonization by becoming strong and modern like Western powers.
Japan wanted to adopt Western culture and become more like Europe.
Japan needed more jobs and believed factories would solve unemployment.
Japan wanted to isolate itself further from the global economy.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following best describes how Muhammad Ali began to industrialize Egypt in the early 1800s?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: He used profits from cotton production to support military and industrial reforms.
He relied on Egypt’s gold and oil exports to fund government factories.
He borrowed money from European banks to build new industries.
He partnered with the Ottoman Empire to build shared factories across the region.

assessment_items.question_content:
**How did Egypt’s political and geographic position create challenges for Muhammad Ali’s industrialization efforts in the early 1800s?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Egypt faced pressure from the Ottoman Empire and European powers.
Egypt’s distance from Europe made it difficult to learn about new technologies.
Egypt’s dependence on cotton made it impossible to grow a strong economy.
Egypt lacked access to ports, which prevented it from engaging in global trade.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Why did Muhammad Ali’s strategy of using cotton production to fund Egypt’s industrialization eventually fail?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Egypt became too dependent on a single export and could not compete globally.
Egypt’s cotton was not valuable on the international market.
Cotton profits were too low to support both the military and factories.
Cotton farming caused widespread environmental damage that halted industry.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What challenge did both Japan and Egypt face when trying to industrialize? _Hint: it is a challenge that Japan managed to overcome, but Egypt did not._**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: limited access to coal and energy sources
poor leadership and political instability
lack of raw materials for industrial production
geographic isolation from global trade routes

assessment_items.question_content:
**Why did European powers, especially Britain and France, show increased interest in Egypt during the 1800s?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Egypt’s control of the Suez Canal gave it strategic importance for global trade.
Egypt was one of the first countries in Africa to develop nuclear weapons.
Egypt had a large population that could be used for labor in European factories.
Egypt’s ancient history made it a center of cultural learning and diplomacy.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following best identifies a lasting consequence of Egypt’s attempt at modernization in the 19th century?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Egypt’s modernization efforts led to massive debt and increased European control.
Egypt successfully avoided European imperialism by building a strong industrial economy.
Egypt became a global leader in manufacturing and colonial expansion.
Egypt chose to remain isolated and avoided contact with foreign powers.
